title: NestJS Editor
content:
NestJS Code Editor

Interactive NestJS Playground

Practice NestJS code directly in your browser! This editor simulates a NestJS environment where you can write Controllers, Providers, and Modules.


What You Can Practice

  • Controllers - Handling incoming requests
  • Providers - Dependency injection & services
  • Modules - Organizing code architecture
  • Middleware - Request/Response manipulation
  • Pipes - Validation & transformation
  • Guards - Authentication & authorization

Pro Tips

  • Use Ctrl + Enter to quickly run your code
  • Controllers must be decorated with @Controller()
  • Services must be decorated with @Injectable()

title: NestJS Introduction
content:
NestJS Introduction

A Progressive Node.js Framework

NestJS is a framework for building efficient, scalable Node.js server-side applications. It uses progressive JavaScript, is built with and fully supports TypeScript, and combines elements of OOP (Object Oriented Programming), FP (Functional Programming), and FRP (Functional Reactive Programming).

Why NestJS?

  • Architecture: Highly organized and modular structure
  • TypeScript: First-class support for better developer experience
  • Versatility: Can be used with Express or Fastify
  • Testability: Built-in dependency injection for easy testing

title: NestJS Installation
content:
NestJS Installation

Nest CLI

The easiest way to start a new NestJS project is by using the Nest CLI.

Terminal Commands
npm i -g @nestjs/cli
nest new project-name
cd project-name
npm run start:dev

title: Controllers
content:
Controllers

Handling Requests

Controllers are responsible for handling incoming requests and returning responses to the client.

app.controller.ts
import { Controller, Get } from '@nestjs/common';

@Controller('cats')
export class CatsController {
  @Get()
  findAll(): string {
    return 'This action returns all cats';
  }
}

// Simulation Execution
const controller = new CatsController();
console.log('Output:', controller.findAll());

title: Providers
content:
Providers

Dependency Injection

Providers are a fundamental concept in Nest. Many of the basic Nest classes may be treated as a provider—services, repositories, factories, helpers, and so on. The main idea of a provider is that it can be injected as a dependency.

cats.service.ts
import { Injectable } from '@nestjs/common';

@Injectable()
export class CatsService {
  private readonly cats: string[] = [];

  create(cat: string) {
    this.cats.push(cat);
  }

  findAll(): string[] {
    return this.cats;
  }
}

// Simulation Execution
const service = new CatsService();
service.create('Siamese');
console.log('Cats:', service.findAll());

title: Modules
content:
Modules

Organizing Code

A module is a class annotated with a @Module() decorator. The @Module() decorator provides metadata that Nest makes use of to organize the application structure.

cats.module.ts
import { Module } from '@nestjs/common';
import { CatsController } from './cats.controller';
import { CatsService } from './cats.service';

@Module({
  controllers: [CatsController],
  providers: [CatsService],
})
export class CatsModule {}

// Simulation Execution
console.log('CatsModule initialized!');

title: Middleware
content:
Middleware

Request & Response Processing

Middleware functions have access to the request and response objects, and the next() middleware function in the application’s request-response cycle.

logger.middleware.ts
import { Injectable, NestMiddleware } from '@nestjs/common';
import { Request, Response, NextFunction } from 'express';

@Injectable()
export class LoggerMiddleware implements NestMiddleware {
  use(req: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction) {
    console.log('Request received...');
    next();
  }
}

// Simulation Execution
const middleware = new LoggerMiddleware();
middleware.use({}, {}, () => console.log('Next middleware called!'));

title: Exception Filters
content:
Exception Filters

Handling Errors

Exception filters are responsible for processing all unhandled exceptions across an application. When an exception is not handled by your application code, it is caught by this layer.

http-exception.filter.ts
import { ExceptionFilter, Catch, ArgumentsHost, HttpException } from '@nestjs/common';

@Catch(HttpException)
export class HttpExceptionFilter implements ExceptionFilter {
  catch(exception: HttpException, host: ArgumentsHost) {
    console.log('Exception caught:', exception.message);
  }
}

// Simulation Execution
const filter = new HttpExceptionFilter();
filter.catch({ message: 'Forbidden' }, {});

title: Pipes
content:
Pipes

Validation & Transformation

Pipes have two typical use cases: transformation (change input data to desired form) and validation (evaluate input data).

validation.pipe.ts
import { PipeTransform, Injectable, ArgumentMetadata, BadRequestException } from '@nestjs/common';

@Injectable()
export class ParseIntPipe implements PipeTransform<string, number> {
  transform(value: string, metadata: ArgumentMetadata): number {
    const val = parseInt(value, 10);
    if (isNaN(val)) {
      throw new Error('Validation failed');
    }
    return val;
  }
}

// Simulation Execution
const pipe = new ParseIntPipe();
console.log('Result:', pipe.transform('42', {}));

title: Guards
content:
Guards

Authentication & Permissions

Guards determine whether a given request will be handled by the route handler or not, depending on certain conditions (like permissions, roles, ACLs, etc.).

auth.guard.ts
import { Injectable, CanActivate, ArgumentsHost } from '@nestjs/common';

@Injectable()
export class AuthGuard implements CanActivate {
  canActivate(context: any): boolean {
    console.log('Checking permissions...');
    return true;
  }
}

// Simulation Execution
const guard = new AuthGuard();
console.log('Access Granted:', guard.canActivate({}));

title: Interceptors
content:
Interceptors

AOP Magic

Interceptors have a set of useful capabilities: bind extra logic before/after method execution, transform the result or exceptions, or extend basic function behavior.

logging.interceptor.ts
import { Injectable, NestInterceptor, ExecutionContext, CallHandler } from '@nestjs/common';

@Injectable()
export class LoggingInterceptor implements NestInterceptor {
  intercept(context: ExecutionContext, next: CallHandler) {
    console.log('Before handling...');
    return 'Response intercepted';
  }
}

// Simulation Execution
const interceptor = new LoggingInterceptor();
console.log(interceptor.intercept({}, { handle: () => ({}) }));

title: Custom Decorators
content:
Custom Decorators

Reusable Metadata

Nest is built around a language feature called decorators. You can create your own custom decorators to keep your code clean and reusable.

user.decorator.ts
import { createParamDecorator, ExecutionContext } from '@nestjs/common';

// Custom decorator definition
const User = (data, ctx) => {
  return { id: 1, name: 'John Doe' };
};

// Simulation Execution
console.log('Fetched User:', User(null, {}));

title: Database integration
content:
Database (TypeORM)

Persistence Layer

Nest supports a wide variety of tools for database integration. TypeORM is the most popular choice for SQL databases.

app.module.ts
import { TypeOrmModule } from '@nestjs/typeorm';

@Module({
  imports: [
    TypeOrmModule.forRoot({
      type: 'mysql',
      host: 'localhost',
      port: 3306,
      username: 'test',
      password: 'test',
      database: 'test',
      entities: [],
      synchronize: true,
    }),
  ],
})
export class AppModule {}

// Simulation Execution
console.log('Database connected successfully!');

title: CRUD Operations
content:
CRUD Operations

Data Management

Implementing Create, Read, Update, and Delete operations using NestJS and TypeORM.

cats.service.ts
@Injectable()
export class CatsService {
  private cats = [];

  async create(cat: any) {
    this.cats.push(cat);
    return cat;
  }

  async findAll() {
    return this.cats;
  }
}

// Simulation Execution
const service = new CatsService();
service.create({ name: 'Tom' });
service.findAll().then(cats => console.log('All Cats:', cats));

title: Microservices
content:
Microservices

Distributed Systems

Nest provides a microservices-style of application development. It supports multiple transport layers like TCP, Redis, RabbitMQ, and Kafka.

main.ts
import { NestFactory } from '@nestjs/core';
import { Transport } from '@nestjs/microservices';

async function bootstrap() {
  console.log('Launching microservice on TCP port 8888...');
  console.log('Success! Listening for messages...');
}
bootstrap();

title: Testing
content:
Testing

Ensuring Quality

Nest provides a great set of tools for testing, built on top of Jest.

app.controller.spec.ts
describe('AppController', () => {
  it('should return "Hello World!"', () => {
    const response = 'Hello World!';
    console.log('Test Passed: Response is "Hello World!"');
  });
});

// Simulation Execution
console.log('Running AppController tests...');
console.log('✓ Test Suite Passed');

title: NestJS Summary
content:
NestJS Course Summary

Key Takeaways

You have learned the basics of NestJS!

  • Architecture: Modular and scalable by design
  • Controllers: Basic request handling
  • Providers: Dependency injection at its core
  • Modules: Organizing your application logic
  • TypeScript: Strong typing for backend applications

Next Steps

  • Learn about TypeORM or Mongoose integration
  • Explore Microservices with NestJS
  • Implement GraphQL APIs
  • Deep dive into Interceptors and Exception Filters

Keep building with NestJS!
